data.title: Economic Reality Enters The Chat Room
data.description:
My regular readers know I have been quite pessimistic about the economy and the markets for well over a year now. The main reason for this outlook was that the market was trading at extreme valuations using various historical valuation metrics (S&P 500 price to sales ratio, Shiller P/E, Market Cap to GDP ratio, etc.). Many of these valuations haven't been experienced since the end of the Internet Boom.

These valuations don't seem to have any basis in economic reality. Remember back in 1999, just before the Internet Bust, the U.S. was in the last year of a four-year period where annual GDP growth was four percent or better annually. The entire federal debt load was less than $6 trillion, less than the country has added in the last three years to the national credit card. The Baby Boomers were in their prime earning years and China was delivering double-digit annual GDP growth as it increasingly integrated into the global economy.

Obviously, none of those conditions exists today. The U.S. is projected to grow 1.5% (or less) in 2025. Both Europe and Japan are experiencing even more anemic economic growth. China has a bevy of its own problems, as I highlighted in depth in a recent article. More than 10,000 Baby Boomers are retiring daily.

I have been early in my view. However, I believe Friday could be a 'red letter day' when the market starts to wake up to the reality I have been spotlighting for some time now. A wave of economic readings pointed to how weak the economy is becoming hit the wires today. Let's go into detail on some of these key readings.

The big economic headline of the day came out an hour before the opening bell today. This was, of course, the BLS Jobs Report. It read that only 73,000 new positions were created in July. This was substantially below the 110,000 consensus. Only one economist surveyed by Bloomberg had a number lower than the actual numbers. More importantly, the prior two BLS monthly job numbers were revised down by a whopping 258,000.

The unemployment rate ticked up .1% from the prior month to 4.2%. In addition, the labor force participation rate fell slightly to 62.2% from 62.3% in June, and below the consensus estimate of 62.4%. Investors should also keep in the back of their mind that based on the pace of Baby Boomer retirements, labor participation is projected to fall to just 58% by 2030. This is a longer-term structural impediment to U.S. economic growth that few are factoring into their economic views. It should be noted that at the tail end of the Internet Boom, the U.S. labor participation rate was just over 67%.

Ironically, this dismal job picture emerges two days after the Federal Reserve once again declined to lower the Fed Funds rate. It should be noted that the decision came with two dissents for the first time since 1953.

Shortly after the opening bell, it was reported that the S&P Global U.S. Manufacturing Purchasing Managers’ Index fell to 49.8 in July from 52.9 in the previous month. Notably, this is the first time this reading has been in recessionary territory (under 50) in 2025.

June Construction Spending also came out Friday and showed an unexpected month-over-month decline of .4%, a slight .1% gain was forecast. In addition, May Construction Spending was revised. I recently posted a piece around the deteriorating housing market and how this trend was going to be an increasing headwind for the U.S. in the months and quarters.

The timing of this spate of economic bad news coincides with a bevy of new tariffs that took effect on August 1st. In summary, investors got some grim news on labor, construction and manufacturing fronts on Friday. This covers a huge chunk of the overall U.S. economy.

This has been a key factor in the yield on the 10-Year Treasury falling by 13 basis points by noon EST today as bondholders factor in that the U.S. economy is significantly weaker than previously thought. Ending on a brighter note, a Fed Funds rate cut in September now seems a near certainty.

My current portfolio is allocated approximately 25% to short-term treasuries and 75% in covered call positions around the few stocks in the market I am finding that sport reasonable valuations. I did move a tad of my cash into the market this morning as the NASDAQ fell by over two percent early on the day, and the S&P VIX Index (VIX) went over 20 for the first time since June on Friday. I will get more aggressive when there is more panic in the market as investors start to bend to economic reality. I fully expect the VIX to spike up to at least 30 this month.

And those are my thoughts around the market on this first day of August, which begins on a sour note and as economic reality starts to enter the chat room.

data.title: PROS Holdings Q2: Subscription Momentum Should Fuel Margin Expansion Ahead
data.description:
The thesis for investing in PROS Holdings (NYSE:PRO) does not revolve around a high flying SaaS growth stock, but rather one where the underlying business is demonstrating consistent double-digit high margin revenue growth, alongside expanding margins. The business continues to benefit from industry tailwinds involving the increased adoption of AI in the CPQ space. In this write-up I look at the opportunities ahead for the business to accelerate its growth rate as well as the risks that investors should consider. Meanwhile, with the stock trading at just 12.5 times next year's FCF, its valuation remains undemanding for a recognized market leader.

Steady Subscription Growth Continues Alongside Strong Operating Leverage

While PROS posted Q2 revenue of $88.7 million, which was up 8% year over year, investors ought to look under the hood at subscription revenue growth in order to gauge the actual performance of the business. In this regard, subscription revenue of $73.3 million, which accounted for 83% of total revenue, grew 12% year over year. This growth was well above the higher end of management's guidance.

As shown above, subscription revenue has gross margins of over 80% associated with it, while services have gross margins of 12%, thus lowering the overall gross margin profile of the company. I therefore focus solely on subscription revenue when it comes to analyzing PROS's growth and margins.

While subscription revenue growth was strong, this strength also translated to the bottom line, with adjusted EBITDA growing 42% year over year. This performance is the result of the inherent operating leverage in the business, as total employee headcount actually fell 1.5% year-over-year. The only negative aspect was that FCF declined from $6.4 million in Q2 2024 to $3.2 million this quarter. This, however, was due to a buildup in accounts receivable, which should reverse in the next two quarters, given that FY 2025 FCF is guided to be $42 million at the midpoint, with just $4.3 million generated in H1 2025.

Looking under the hood, key metrics such as gross retention remained healthy at 93%, while total deferred revenue was up 12% year over year, implying strong revenue growth ahead. Management's Q3 guidance calls for 12% year over year subscription revenue growth, which continues to be in line with the CPQ market growth, which research reports suggest is forecasted to grow at a 12% CAGR until 2033.

Given these growth expectations, PRO stock trades at 2.5 times subscription revenue and 17.8 times FY 2025 FCF. Nevertheless, assuming 12% subscription revenue growth in FY 2026, I expect FCF as a percentage of subscription revenue to expand from 14% to nearly 18%, given that at least 50% of incremental subscription revenue in FY 2026 should be converted to FCF. This would translate to FCF of $60 million in FY 2026 and a price to FCF of just 12.5.

The valuation of PRO stock represents a substantial discount to other high-margin SaaS peers with comparable growth rates, such as Amplitude (AMPL), which trades at five times revenue, and Okta (OKTA), which trades at over 20 times FCF. Needless to say, given its size and cheap valuation, PROS remains an attractive acquisition candidate for larger competitors or even private equity.

Growth Opportunities and Risks in Focus

The ability for PROS to accelerate subscription revenue growth from current rates and outpace the CPQ market growth hinges on management's go-to-market efforts paying off. This involves implementing a tighter alignment between marketing and sales as well as forging strategic platform partnerships like the one with BigCommerce (BIGC), where the two companies will combine to offer the CPQ product to B2B merchants on the BigCommerce platform.

While PROS operates in a highly competitive market, dominated by larger players like Oracle (ORCL) and SAP (SAP), who are able to bundle CPQ with other products that they offer, a recent shift by Salesforce (CRM) may open up new opportunities. Notably, Salesforce is sunsetting its CPQ product and replacing it with a Revenue Cloud, a more comprehensive yet more expensive product. For customers seeking a less complex and more cost-effective alternative, PROS stands out as a compelling option.

Following the recent debt refinancing, the company has a solid balance sheet. Though net debt stood at $133 million at the of Q2, implying a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of close to 3, a major portion of it only comes due in 2030. Given the strong FCF expected, I expect rapid deleveraging ahead.

PROS Stock Remains a Buy

The company continues to post healthy double digit subscription revenue growth along with margin expansion, helped by industry tailwinds. Given its high incremental margins, the stock trading at just 12.5 times next year's FCF. I find it too cheaply priced compared to relevant peers, motivating me to retain my Buy rating on the stock.

data.title: VONE: Broadening Exposure To Large Cap Stocks
data.description:
With the S&P 500 trading at or near all-time high levels, there is often temptation to take profits and pull money out of the market. Yet, history shows that all-time highs can actually be good for investors for an extended period of time. Dating back to September of 1989, the S&P 500 has outperformed in the one, three, and five-year periods following an all-time high when compared to all other periods.

Investors who want to participate in the current market environment but may be looking for some modest diversification among a broader range of large-cap stocks may want to consider the Vanguard Russell 1000 ETF (NASDAQ:VONE). The fund seems to check a lot of the right boxes with the low management fees that you expected from a Vanguard index fund. In large part due to its minimal expenses, VONE has outperformed its closest ETF peer, the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), in almost every period in the last decade.

For investors looking to allocate more resources to the current market and diversify some holdings away from just a standard S&P 500 index fund, I rate the Vanguard Russell 1000 ETF as a Buy.

ETF Description

According to Vanguard, its Russell 100 ETF aims to follow the performance of the Russell 1000 Index, an index that tracks the large-cap segment of the US market. The passively managed fund includes stocks from approximately 1000 of the largest publicly traded companies in the US, which represent 93% of the investible US stocks when measured by market capitalization. According to Vanguard, its ETF holds all stocks in the same weighting as the index and "uses proprietary software to implement trading decisions that accommodate cash flow and maintain close correlation with index characteristics."

As of July 31, the fund had $8.9 billion in assets under management and included 1018 stocks in its portfolio. Only three stocks composed more than 4% of the fund: NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Apple (AAPL). The weights from this trio of mega-cap tech stocks are slightly lower in VONE than they would be in an S&P 500 index fund. The difference is most noticeable for NVDA (6.44% in VONE versus 8.1% in the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust).

A full listing of the top 10 holdings for VONE is given below. The top seven stocks on the list are the same as they would be in the S&P 500, albeit with a smaller share in the ETF. Alphabet Class C shares (GOOG) are eighth in the S&P 500 but tenth in VONE. Tesla (TSLA) is tenth in the S&P 500 but ninth in VONE.

According to Morningstar, VONE has almost a 50/50 balance between growth and value stocks. Yet when compared to Morningstar's own index for large-cap blend funds, the VONE portfolio has a slightly lower price/book ratio, price/sales ratio, and price/cash flow ratio. Stocks included in VONE also have a higher long-term earnings growth record, although the difference is only two basis points.

When looking at sector allocation, it should be no surprise that technology holds the top spot in the fund with a 36.6% share. The next closest sector is consumer discretionary at 15%. Industrials are third at 12.6%. Financials is the only other sector that holds a share in double digits.

Dividends are not a great strength for VONE, and therefore the fund is unlikely to attract many income investors. The fund currently yields 1.13%, which is in line with the payout from SPY and 0.05% better than the dividend yield of IWB. VONE's most recent dividend of 0.7828 per share was paid at the end of June. This was 3.8% lower than the payout from the same period in 2024, but since 2022, VONE has been able to grow its dividend by an average of 8.1% per year.

Fund Analysis

One of the biggest advantages of an investment in VONE is the fund's ability to offer diversification across the large-cap space with a low expense ratio. VONE charges just 0.07% to its investors, which is a savings of 0.12% over its closest competitor, IWB. Over 20 years with an investment of $10,000, the difference in fees alone would add just over $220 to the account of a VONE shareholder.

Of course, higher fees are worth paying if investors are getting a higher return. However, that is not the case with IWB. While the iShares version of the index fund does have an advantage year-to-date of 0.01%, VONE has a better performance over the six-month, three-year, five-year, and 10-year periods. The difference is not a large one, and in many cases, the difference in performance is within the range of the difference in the two funds' management fees. Nonetheless, these differences could be meaningful for long-term investors.

At first glance, some of the internal metrics for VONE should create concern for risk-averse investors. Vanguard states that the average P/E ratio for the fund is 27. This is unquestionably high by historical standards. However, Vanguard also says the fund has an earnings growth rate of 21.5%, giving the fund a much more reasonable estimated PEG ratio of 1.26.

In addition to my own positive assessment of VONE, the fund receives high marks from other financial rating sources. Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating declares VONE as a Buy with high marks in each of its five categories. It is no surprise that the fund gets an "A+" in expenses. The fund also gets a grade of "A" for liquidity and "A-" for momentum and risk. The high liquidity score is based on the fund's total assets under management as well as the average daily volume traded in the fund over the last three months. The high score for risk is not directly related to the stock portfolio for the fund itself, but rather the low tracking error for the fund over the last one, three, and five-year periods.

Morningstar also gives the fund four stars and a silver rating, designating the ETF as one that is likely to outperform its peer group on a risk-adjusted basis. Over the last decade, the fund has significantly outperformed Morningstar's large cap blend category over the last decade, even as it trailed its own large cap index slightly.

Zacks gives VONE a Buy-2 rating, which is the equivalent of a buy rating. The Zacks rating is intended to predict the timeliness of a current purchase in the fund for the next six months.

Risks to Consider

An investment in VONE is certainly not without risk, and there are certainly a number of sources currently warning that the market is due for a correction. By one measure, the largest companies in the Russell 1000 index are more overvalued than the top companies during the peak of the dot-com bubble. The top 10 stocks by market cap are trading around 25 times forward earnings. Another article that appeared on Seeking Alpha on July 7 used the Buffett Indicator to signal signs of an overextended market. As the author stated:

The Buffett indicator is calculated as total market capitalization/GDP. When this value blows up, it indicates the stock market optimism is overshooting the rate of GDP growth. The figure below illustrates the trend in the Buffett indicator, with the current value standing at 209.5%. By standard conventions, any value beyond 160% is considered as indicating overvaluation.

The article also referenced record levels of household ownership of corporate equities and mutual funds as signs that a market pullback is warranted.

Vanguard refers to VONE risk assessment as "moderate to aggressive" and notes that investors should be prepared for "wide fluctuations" in the share price of the ETF. Funds like VONE are most appropriate for investors who are trying to reach long-term goals. The chance of a significant loss in the fund in the short term is significant. The fund does not generate enough dividends to be considered a reasonable option for income-focused investors.

For most investors, VONE would best be held in a tax-deferred account. According to Vanguard, as of June 30, over 37.5% of the fund's net asset value is comprised of unrealized gains. While the annual turnover in the account is quite low (e.g., 2.6% in 2024), investors are still exposed to the potential for higher tax consequences over a longer period of time.

The Bottom Line

In conclusion, I rate the Vanguard Russell 1000 ETF as a Buy with the following caveats:

  • The fund is a good option for investors with a long-term investment horizon. I would recommend at least a five-year period. Vanguard's fund disclaimers reference a full ten years. Dollar cost averaging could be a good way to help mitigate some of the risks mentioned above.
  • The fund is a good option for investors who want to participate in the growth of mega-cap technology stocks like NVIDIA and Microsoft without quite the same risk exposure as the S&P 500. The slightly lower portfolio allocations to those stocks provide at least a small buffer to the elevated risk associated with those companies.
  • Expense fees matter, and VONE provides access to this broader group of large-cap stocks at a lower cost than the iShares Russell 1000 ETF. Investors should always consider expenses when selecting ETFs for investing.

For investors looking for low-cost, diversified, large-cap investing options, VONE is a strong choice.

data.title: Okta: Fair Valuation With High Speculative Upside
data.description:
Okta, Inc. (NASDAQ:OKTA) is one of the companies that saw their valuation surge during the pandemic but could never fully recover from the drop in 2022. With growth slowing down significantly and no particular catalysts, there had been no compelling reason to buy the stock until now.

Looking at Okta's recent numbers, the company still does not look particularly exciting. However, potential AI-driven catalysts, fair valuation, and stable financials might make Okta a potential acquisition target, especially in light of recent speculations. This makes the stock an interesting investment with a solid risk/reward opportunity, which is why I initiate OKTA with a Buy rating.

With strong business and solid market share, Okta is a compelling acquisition target

One of Okta's strongest points is its promising business and solid market share.

According to research, the company had about 27% market share in the identity-as-a-service market in 2024, with more than 19,000 customers. In the single-sign-on market, the company holds about 33% market share with almost 28,000 customers. And drawing from my own experience, almost every company I worked at used Okta for identity and access management.

Significant market share should help Okta in two major ways. Firstly, the company should have strong pricing power in the future and be able to gradually raise prices to improve its margins. Secondly, a high number of customers enables the company to generate vast amounts of data that could be valuable for training AI models focused on security.

And with AI technology developing fast, security will inevitably become an even more important topic in the medium term. Algorithms can process huge amounts of data much faster and in a more focused way than humans or even traditional computing. This is why "AI-native" cybersecurity is gradually becoming a new normal, and Okta is already emphasizing its Okta AI solutions for enhanced security.

The relevance of the issue can also be seen in the stock market. Cybersecurity stocks like Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) or CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) have seen their shares surge by more than 300% in the last five years. In the M&A space, Palo Alto has recently agreed to acquire CyberArk Software Ltd. (CYBR), which is an identity security company somewhat similar to Okta. Interestingly, just a couple of weeks prior to that, Palo Alto and Okta expanded their partnership in AI-driven security solutions.

I expect this trend to continue or even accelerate in the near future. There have already been takeover speculations this week around Okta becoming a takeover target, which led to the stock jumping about 5%. Even if these particular rumors do not materialize in the short term, Okta's promising business, AI exposure, and somewhat affordable market share make the company a very real acquisition target, in my opinion.

Okta's financial performance resembles that of a mature company

I find Okta's financial performance rather interesting here, as it can be viewed from two sides.

On the one hand, despite the fact that Okta is a high-potential tech company that used to grow rapidly just a couple of years ago, its numbers now resemble those of a more mature, less interesting company. Okta's quarterly revenue growth has slowed down from 50-60% in 2021-2022 to just 11.5% in the most recent quarter. Compared to FQ1 2025, the company also showed stable sales and marketing expenses and declining research and development investments in FQ1 2026. I find the latter especially unusual for a tech security company in 2025.

On the other hand, this stability and emphasis on profitability can be viewed as a positive. Okta has a strong balance sheet with about $2.7 billion in cash and short-term investments and about $350 million in long-term debt in the form of convertible notes. The company also generated about $241 million in cash from operating activities in the most recent quarter. This means Okta is not likely to face any significant financial risks in the medium term, and it also improves its profile as a potential acquisition target.

OKTA stock is fairly valued

Okta also seems to be one of the more fairly valued stocks on the market. OKTA now trades at around the lowest forward P/E ratio in years, which might be partially justified by its slowing revenue growth. Still, with an estimated 3-year average earnings growth of about 13% and a long history of surpassing expectations, the valuation ratios seem, at the very least, adequate.

If Okta's actual earnings growth edges closer to 15-20%, the stock could easily trade at around 30-40 forward P/E in the coming years, considering the industry average PEG ratio of 1.5-2. This could lead to a stock price of around $114-152 by the end of 2026 — not the best margin of safety on the lower end, but no signs of overvaluation either.

Comparing Okta to some of its peers, the company looks like one of the most attractive stocks. OKTA's forward P/E ratios are several times lower than those of most competitors. And slow growth does not fully explain this low valuation, as Okta's P/E ratio relative to growth (PEG) is still the lowest among its peers.

And diving into growth specifically, Okta is not far behind most of its peers, especially on longer time frames. Its 5-year revenue CAGR of 33% is one of the highest, with only Datadog, Inc. (DDOG) ahead. The metric is also higher than that of more security-focused companies like CyberArk or Palo Alto Networks. So here again, Okta's low valuation seems like a potential bargain.

Ratings

Seeking Alpha's rating system gives Okta a moderate Buy rating with 3.4-3.9 grades. The only negative grade here is for valuation.

At the same time, looking at Okta's valuation grade in more detail, it appears that it is skewed toward negative primarily by its GAAP multiples. These are usually not very informative when it comes to growth-focused tech companies, especially when they have high stock-based compensation costs, which are non-cash expenses. Okta's aforementioned low Non-GAAP PEG ratio shines here again with a positive B rating.

Technical chart

From a technical analysis view, Okta has been trading in a narrow trend range, which seems to have established itself already in 2018. Importantly, the current stock price is almost exactly in the middle. Without additional catalysts, the stock might just continue trending slowly upward, with limited upside but also limited downside, making it a more stable investment.

Okta might have a high speculative upside

What makes Okta's stock more compelling, though, is its potential to become an acquisition or a buyout target. Just recently, Palo Alto Networks paid a 26% premium to acquire CyberArk shares. From my view, a potential premium for Okta would be even higher as the company's shares are valued much lower than CyberArk's before the acquisition.

In general, the average merger premium is estimated to be around 34%, with the average high-tech industry premium at 89%. If Okta gets acquired, the stock might quickly jump to somewhere around $130-180. This is clearly more of a speculative assumption, but I believe it is warranted in light of the recent rumors.

Key takeaways

Overall, judging solely by Okta's fundamental performance, I don't see particularly huge upside potential for the stock at the moment. With slowing growth and an established chart pattern, the somewhat low valuation multiples seem fair — limited room for growth but also no signs of overvaluation. So, at the minimum, the stock should be able to grow just slightly faster than the market in the medium term.

However, with recent acquisition rumors and the general tendency for mergers in the cybersecurity market at the moment, Okta becomes a more interesting investment. Its reasonable valuation and AI-driven potential might make Okta a solid target, which could easily lead to a buyer paying a 30-80% premium. Therefore, with somewhat low risk and high potential reward, I give Okta a Buy rating.

data.title: SMDV: A Sector-Heavy Play On Small-Cap Dividend Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (BATS:SMDV) on June 3, 2024, when I recommended readers avoid it due to its unappealing factor mix and high concentration. Since that article was published, SMDV managed to perform pretty well against its small-cap value peers. As shown below, its 8.35% total return almost matched the 8.40% gain for the iShares Russell 2000 Value ETF (IWN), beat DES and DGRS, two other WisdomTree small-cap dividend funds, and outpaced the Avantis US Small Cap Value ETF (AVUV) by 3.54%.

Still, that doesn't really change my opinion about the fund over the long term. As I'll demonstrate below, SMDV's returns over the last six years were far from impressive, and its fundamentals remain weak, with the market's appetite for lower-quality stocks likely fueling its solid returns over the last year. In my view, that won't last long, so I'm reiterating my recommendation to avoid SMDV, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

SMDV Overview

Strategy Discussion

SMDV has a 0.40% expense ratio and $648 million in AUM. It tracks the Russell 2000 Dividend Growth Index, selecting Russell 2000 constituents with at least ten consecutive years of dividend growth. The following is a summary of the selection process based on this document.

1. The Index excludes the bottom 20% of Russell 2000 securities based on their 20-day average dollar traded volume. The data cut-off date is the last business day of May.

2. Eligible securities are assessed annually for dividend growth over seven quarters as of the last business day in May. All Index securities must have at least ten consecutive years of increasing dividend payments.

3. The Index equal weights all securities but places a 30% weight limit for each sector using the Russell Global Sector classification system. The years of consecutive dividend growth act as the tiebreaker, followed by dividend yield.

4. The Index is reconstituted annually on the fourth Friday in June.

The strategy is pretty straightforward but strongly favors the Financials sector, as there are dozens of small-cap regional banks on 10+ year dividend growth streaks. Industrials and Utilities are also popular, so the first takeaway is that the Index is designed to be concentrated in these three sectors.

Performance Snapshot

We saw earlier how SMDV has done well over the last year, but longer term, it's struggled against those same four peers. As shown below, SMDV has only delivered a total return (including dividends) of 28.26% since AVUV's launch on September 24, 2019. DES, DGRS, and IWN easily outperformed, while AVUV was the big winner with a 104.56% total return.

In fairness, SMDV's returns have been much better since its inception. As shown below, it produced a terrific 35.21% total return in 2016 and held up far better than DES, DGRS, and IWN in 2018, declining by just 0.60%. Similarly, SMDV declined by 5-9% less in 2022, so it does seem to do a good job in market downturns. This analysis highlights a maximum drawdown figure that's favorable by about 9-14%, and a much stronger Sortino Ratio, a standard measure of downside risk-adjusted returns, compared to those three peers. The only catch is that the Sortino Ratio advantage disappears with the five-year backtest, so it's a question of which data is most relevant, and for that, I prefer to rely on fundamental analysis.

SMDV Analysis

Sector Allocations and Size Segment Distribution

The following tables highlight the sector allocation and size segment distribution differences between SMDV, DES, DGRS, AVUV, and IWN. We see that SMDV favors the Financials, Industrials, and Utilities sectors, and it's crucial to know that this will be the case for the foreseeable future, since the vast majority of Russell 2000 Index stocks with 10+ dividend growth years are from these sectors. Overall, SMDV's sector diversification is similar to DGRS and AVUV but much worse than DES and IWN, so if you are looking for a "core" small-cap holding, those two are probably better choices.

From a size segment distribution perspective, all five ETFs are reasonably similar, with all holdings falling under the "small" and "micro" segments, representing the bottom 10% by cumulative market cap. That makes them good comparators and ensures all could act as possible complements to your existing large/mid-cap holdings.

SMDV Dividend Snapshot

SMDV current yields 2.64% and is on a four-year dividend growth streak, though that streak will likely stop, given its -8.64% TTM dividend growth rate. Its three- and five-year dividend growth rates of 6.73% and 6.41% are pretty solid, but I want to point out that the 19.56% ten-year rate is inaccurate. SMDV launched a little over ten years ago on February 3, 2015, but to calculate a ten-year growth rate, we need 11 years of history. As such, I expect SMDV's "A" Dividend Grade to deteriorate as the year progresses.

SMDV Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for SMDV's top 25 sub-industries, which comprise 84.46% of the portfolio. Compared to the four peers, this concentration level is quite high, and since regional banks make up one quarter of the fund, it's certainly not a well-diversified fund despite the Index's 30% sector weight caps.

Here are three additional observations to consider:

1. SMDV has a 0.84 five-year portfolio beta, indicating a relatively low level of market risk that's unusual for the small-cap space. That aligns with the solid drawdown performance we saw earlier, but shareholders must balance that with SMDV's poor profitability metrics. The table also notes its sector-adjusted profit score, which I derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, is the lowest of the four at 4.97/10. That corresponds to a little better than a "C" grade, and although that's in line with IWN's score, AVUV is the highest quality fund of the five. Avantis managers specifically screen for quality, and I offer that as a key reason why it has outperformed its peers over the long run.

2. SMDV's Index yield is 3.30%, and after subtracting the fund's 0.40% expense ratio, shareholders should net 2.90% at current prices. DES offers a slightly better expected net yield of 3.26% (3.64% - 0.38%), while income investors probably won't have much interest in the yields of DGRS, AVUV, or IWN. That said, it's hard to make an income case for any of these ETFs unless your goal is to generate income from every one of your investments. You're better off sticking with higher-quality large-cap ETFs for dividends, many of which yield above 3%, and use the small-cap sector for diversification and potential growth purposes.

3. On that note, SMDV's growth prospects are the best of the five, which might help explain its recent success. The analysis table shows a double-digit one-year estimated earnings growth rate for its regional bank selections, which is far above their 0.33% three-year historical earnings growth rates. Overall, SMDV's components score the highest on these two metrics, and although its 14.39x forward P/E ratio is slightly more expensive, it's competitive from a GARP perspective, as follows:

  • SMDV: 14.39x / 7.44% = 1.93x.
  • DES: 13.03x / 7.01% = 1.86x.
  • DGRS: 13.65x / 6.38% = 2.14x.
  • AVUV: 11.79x / 4.49% = 2.63x.
  • IWN: 13.04x / 5.26% = 2.48x.

Still, you might prefer DES for its superior diversification, higher dividend yield, and the lower PEG ratio from above. Interestingly, it's AVUV that looks the weakest on that last metric, so although it hits the value and quality factors better than its peers, I'm cautious about investing in any ETF with such meager growth.

Investment Recommendation

My opinion of SMDV has slightly improved since my review one year ago, but my reservations about the fund's poor diversification, unappealing yield, and low quality remain. Defensive investors might have some success, given the fund's low 0.84 five-year portfolio beta and its solid history of outperforming in market downturns. However, if it's dividend growth you're after, better-quality large-cap funds with higher starting yields and lower expense ratios are your better bet. Therefore, I've assigned a "hold" rating on SMDV, but I look forward to reading your thoughts in the comments section below.

data.title: Engie: Sustained Growth Potential, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY) (OTCPK:ENGQF) released H1 2025 numbers this morning. At first glance, Engie’s H1 2025 results appear to be underwhelming. However, we maintain a neutral outlook on the release, viewing the company’s reaffirmation of its guidance as a positive sign. For our new readers, Engie is a French-based global utility company that produces, supplies, and sells electricity, gas, and energy services. Engie has successfully transformed its business, shifting its focus towards more sustainable and diversified energy solutions, and our buy rating is backed by its 1) Upside on the renewable energy capacity growth; 2) Infrastructure-regulated business that offers downside protection; 3) A discount to peers in terms of valuation; and 4) A group strategy of simplification and cost savings.

Since our rating upgrade (Q1 2024—Fig. 1), the company’s shares have gained more than 30% (for a total return of more than 55%), and we interpret today's adverse stock price reaction as an opportunity for investors to potentially profit.

Fig 1

H1 Results and Our Positive Take

Starting with reporting results, Engie delivered sales of €38.1 billion, which was up by 2.9% organically. FX and scope negatively impacted €98 million and €80 million, respectively. The latter was mainly due to the disposal of Uch (Pakistan), Safi (Morocco), and Senoko (Singapore). The company reported an H1 25 core operating profit (ex. nuclear) of €5.1 billion (Fig. 2), which aligned with the Wall Street consensus. By division, it reported positive performance of the Networks segment, which was offset by the negative results of renewables and BESS (impacted by lower hydro volumes), gas generation (impacted by lower spreads), and B2B and B2C energy prices (normalization trends). Engie reported a net income of €3.1 billion (compared to a €3.8 billion result in H1 2024), with an adverse effect on higher than expected tax and higher minority interests.

Fig 2

Source: Engie H1 results ppt

Despite these results, we remain cautiously optimistic and are not altering our positive outlook for the company moving forward. This is based on the following:

  1. In H1 2025, the infrastructure core operating profit increased by 38% year-on-year to almost €2 billion (Fig. 3). New supply tariffs in LATAM and Europe mainly supported this. This recurring profit offers clear downside protection linked to a regulatory asset-based remuneration. While energy spread might be cyclical, this division provides downside protection. On an annual basis, with a core operating profit of at least €4.2 billion and applying a similar valuation as Terna/E.ON, a FWD EV/EBIT multiple of 10x, this segment accounts for more than half of the company’s enterprise value.
  2. Despite subdued results in the Supply & Energy Management and Renewables & Flex Power EBIT, the company has reiterated its 2025 guidance with a 2025 EBIT (ex-nuclear) of €8.0-9.0 billion and net profit of €4.4-5.0 billion (Fig. 2). The guidance also reflects the commodity outlook as of the end of June 2025, with minor changes. In detail, Engie reported an effective higher tax rate guidance of 23-25% (vs a prior estimate of 22-24%) and a lower recurring net financial cost of €2.0-2.2 billion (vs. a previous forecast of €2.1-2.3 billion).
  3. During the call, the CEO expressed uncertainty about the US clients' willingness to pay a higher PPA price (due to higher renewable energy costs). The company flagged the option to reallocate CAPEX to other regions. At this stage, we report that H1 gross CAPEX reached €3.3 billion and was down compared to last year. Engie also anticipates a preference to allocate CAPEX to the Networks division, which for us is supportive evidence for multiple re-rating and higher downside protection. Additionally (Fig. 2), the company's economic net debt was down €1.1 billion vs year-end (despite the dividend payment and lower operating cash flow). This was due to a favorable working capital release.

Fig 3

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Engie has maintained its 2025 outlook, so we have made no adjustments to our estimates (EBITDA and net profit projections of €13.5 billion and €4.45 billion, respectively).

Engie possesses upside potential that we are not currently factoring in, including the possibility of multiple re-ratings, favorable EU legislation supporting renewable energy growth, and higher recurring earnings from its network division, which accounts for 40% of the current EBIT (and as reported above in a sum-of-the-part valuation might account for at least 50% of the total company's enterprise value). Additionally, although we expect the company to reduce its CAPEX, leading to a lower economic net debt, we maintain our year-end forecast of €50 billion.

Going to the valuation, continuing to apply a discount vs the broader sector, we used a blended methodology with a target P/E of 10x and an EV/EBITDA of 8x. With the current outstanding shares, we derive an EPS of €1.83 (valuing the company at €18.3 per share). With the EV/EBITDA, our equity value reached €58 billion, deriving a share price of €23.7. Blending the valuation, our target price is €21 per share ($23.9 in ENGIY ADR). Our buy rating is confirmed.

Risks

Engie downside risks include: 1) Energy price normalization (gas and electricity) with lower spread; 2) CAPEX delay (as happened in Q2 2025); 3) Government issues since the company is owned (23%) in part by the French government; 4) Lower dividends; 5) Possible shifts in sustainability goals: 6) Given the economic net debt, higher interest rates impact the company P&L; 7) Higher regulatory requirements; and 8) As happened in the H1 2025 guidance change, the company might be impacted by higher taxes.

Conclusion

Engie confirmed its 2025 guidance with minor changes. The company's upside is still there and materializing, such as the higher RAB contract in the network division. For this reason, we remain buyers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CRH Solidifying Its Industry Leadership With $2.1b Acquisition
data.description:
CRH plc (NYSE:CRH) is laying the groundwork for substantial growth by making major acquisitions year-to-date, significantly expanding its geographical presence and materials supplies. With durable expectations for infrastructure projects through IIJA funding and increasing demand for materials for reindustrialization and data center construction, CRH should be well-positioned to realize volume growth paired with appealing material pricing. Given the appealing economic dynamics at hand, I am recommending CRH with a Buy rating with a price target of $106/share at 10.79x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

CRH Operations

CRH faced minor headwinds throughout Q1’25 as a result of adverse weather conditions, impacting activity across the organization. Despite the headwinds, total revenue grew by 3%, driven by strong International Solutions. Materials pricing had a positive effect on revenue for the quarter, with cement pricing increasing by 4%; aggregate prices across all materials increased by 8%.

CRH has been heavily engaged in M&A activity. Small, bolt-on acquisitions are part of CRH’s overall growth strategy, given the fragmentation of the market. Through aggregating smaller suppliers into a single entity, I believe CRH will have the capabilities of realizing pricing and margin improvements paired with higher volume sales across more regions. Accordingly, management is anticipating net contributions to adjusted EBITDA to be roughly $320mm as a result of bolt-on acquisitions for eFY25.

The organization executed 8 bolt-on acquisitions valued at $585mm throughout Q1’25. Accordingly, the acquisitions had a cumulative net asset value of $246mm, generating $340mm in goodwill. The companies acquired align with CRH’s overall business strategy, expanding its presence in essential materials, road solutions, critical infrastructure, and outdoor living.

On July 29, 2025, CRH announced the intent to acquire Eco Material Technologies for $2.1b with the expectation of the deal closing in 2025. Eco Materials is a leading provider of supplementary cement materials made from fly ash and other pozzolanic materials in North America. The acquisition is expected to enhance CRH’s supply of critical materials as well as its national distribution network. According to Eco Material’s website, the organization serves over 4,000 unique customers across 45 states. Eco Materials will bring in 125 utility source locations, production facilities, and terminals. Eco Materials also has strategic partnerships with electric utilities to process and recycle fly ash and synthetic gypsum from coal combustion. This could be a major growth business following the recent EPA announcement relating to extending the deadline for coal combustion residual management unit requirements.

CRH’s infrastructure business is somewhat dependent on funding through the Infrastructure Investment & Jobs Act [IIJA]. Management anticipates state and federal funding to continue supporting growth in CRH’s infrastructure business for eFY25. Accordingly, roughly 1/3rd of the total funding has been spent, paving a long runway for CRH to capitalize on funded infrastructure projects.

From a macro perspective, CRH has two major tailwinds coming into play: reindustrialization and data centers. McKinsey forecasts that the data center footprint will grow to 156GW in capacity by 2030, a 125GW increase from 2025 to 2030. Industrial manufacturing should also be positioned for substantial growth through manufacturing reshoring following the imposed U.S. trade tariffs. Both of these markets can drive substantial demand for cement and asphalt for buildings and infrastructure.

CRH Financial Position

CRH has been heavily reinvesting in the organization through M&A and capital investments. Capital investments have historically sat in the mid-to-high single digits as a percentage of revenue, with the last two quarters being at 11% and 10%, respectively. I’m forecasting CRH to invest $2.9b in eFY25 while generating $2.7b in free cash flow. Between the heightened investment outlay and the $585mm in M&A, CRH has built up a substantial debt position of $15.7b for a leverage ratio of 1.76x net debt/aEBITDA.

Looking ahead to Q2’25, I’m forecasting CRH to generate $10.1b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $2.05/share. With management optimistic and pricing dynamics going forward paired with the appealing economic landscape, I believe CRH should realize margin-accretive revenue growth, resulting in strong net earnings growth.

Risks Related to CRH

Bull Case

CRH is undergoing substantial growth through M&A, with a large deal being announced in July 2025. Given the industrial manufacturing and data center markets’ growth expectations for the duration of the decade, CRH should be well-positioned as a key national materials supplier, potentially driving stronger pricing and volume growth.

Bear Case

The tariff environment has created substantial economic uncertainty, pushing out construction projects as a result of potential material pricing impacts. This could impact CRH indirectly and may slow growth in its Building and Infrastructure Solutions market segment.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

CRH has been heavily involved in returning value to shareholders through its robust dividend distribution and share repurchases. In Q1’25, CRH repurchased roughly $500mm in shares and authorized an additional $300mm for Q2’25. The organization also increased its dividend distribution by 6% in Q’25 to $0.37/share, annualized at $1.48/share with a forward yield of 1.67%.

Placing a value on CRH shares, I believe shares should be priced at $106/share at 10.79x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA; I am recommending CRH shares with a BUY rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.
